title: Bamia
subtitle: Lebanese okra and lamb stew in tomato sauce
excerpt: Small okra pods cooked with lamb shoulder and a fragrant tomato sauce seasoned with allspice and cinnamon. The key to good bamia is using whole small okra, briefly pan-frying it before adding to the sauce, and not stirring too aggressively — okra that is cut or overworked releases its viscous sap and the stew becomes slimy.
sourceNotes: Bamia (from the Arabic word for okra) is a Levantine stew documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) and eaten across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, and Egypt. The Lebanese version typically uses lamb or beef and a lightly spiced tomato sauce. A vegetarian version made with chickpeas is also common.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked stew for up to 3 months. Reheat gently — the okra may soften further.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook a day ahead and reheat on a low simmer for 15 minutes. The flavour improves overnight.

content.text: Okra has a reputation for sliminess that comes from cutting the pods, overhandling them, or cooking them in too much liquid. Whole small pods, briefly fried in oil before going into the sauce, stay intact and release far less of their natural mucilage. The frying also adds a slight char that deepens the flavour. Use the smallest okra you can find — larger pods are tougher and take longer to cook. Frozen whole okra is a good substitute and behaves well in this stew.

The lamb shoulder is cut into large pieces and browned before the tomato sauce is added. It needs 45 to 50 minutes of simmering to become tender. The okra goes in for the last 15 minutes only — adding it earlier results in overcooked pods that fall apart. Serve with plain rice and pitta.

items.prepNote: small pods, tops trimmed but caps intact

content.text: Fry the okra and brown the lamb

Heat 2 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large pan over high heat. Fry the {{okra}} in a single layer for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until lightly coloured. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, add the remaining oil and brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces in batches over high heat, about 3 to 4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} to the pan and cook for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}}, {{allspice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}}, and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and 200 ml water. Season with salt. Return the lamb to the pan, bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 45 minutes until the lamb is tender. Add the fried {{okra}} and the {{lemon}} juice and cook uncovered for a further 15 minutes.

items.cause: Okra cut or added too early and overcooked
items.fix: Only trim the very tip of the stem cap — do not cut into the pod. Add the okra for the last 15 minutes only and do not stir vigorously

items.symptom: Lamb is still tough after 45 minutes
items.cause: Pieces too large or heat too low
items.fix: Cut pieces to 5 cm maximum. Check the simmer is active — a lid-on low simmer is often not hot enough. Add 15 to 20 minutes more if needed
Cook uncovered for the final 15 minutes to reduce. If still thin after the okra is cooked, remove the okra and reduce the sauce separately
Check at 12 minutes — okra is done when it is tender but still holding its shape. Pull it from the heat and rest it in the residual heat of the sauce

content.text: Bamia belongs to the large family of Levantine lamb-and-vegetable stews that form the backbone of everyday cooking in Lebanon, Syria, and Palestine. The word 'bamia' is the Arabic name for okra, which arrived in the region from Africa. Similar stews appear across Egypt and North Africa. In Lebanon it is a comfortable home dish, made in large quantities and served over rice, rarely on a restaurant menu but always present in domestic cooking. The combination of okra, lamb, and tomato with allspice and cinnamon is distinctly Levantine, distinguishing it from the North African versions that lean toward cumin and coriander.
